private lemma iotaGm_isOpenImmersion [IsAlgClosed kbar] :
    IsOpenImmersion ((lift (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar)
        (𝟙 (Gm kbar))).left ≫ (gmScalingP1 kbar).left) := by
  -- Step (a): unfold `(lift _ _).left` to `pullback.lift _ _ _` via `Over.lift_left`,
  -- and expose `gmScalingP1.left` as its `glueMorphisms` form.
  rw [Over.lift_left]
  simp only [Over.comp_left, Over.id_left, Over.toUnit_left]
  change IsOpenImmersion (pullback.lift _ _ _ ≫
    (gmScalingP1_cover kbar).glueMorphisms (gmScalingP1_chart kbar)
      (gmScalingP1_chart_agreement kbar))
  -- Step (b): obtain the chart-1 factorisation `r_1` of `onePt.left`.
  obtain ⟨r_1, h_r_1⟩ := iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor kbar
  -- Step (c)+(d)+(e): build the section `s` and identify
  -- `pullback.lift _ _ _ ≫ glueMorphisms = s ≫ gmScalingP1_chart 1` via
  -- `Cover.ι_glueMorphisms` applied to chart `1 : Fin 2`. The section is the
  -- `pullback.lift` of two components into `(cover).X 1 = pullback q (awayι (X 1))`.
  rw [show pullback.lift ((Gm kbar).hom ≫ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left)
        (𝟙 (Gm kbar).left)
        (by
          have hov : (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left ≫
              (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom =
              (𝟙_ (Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)))).hom :=
            Over.w (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar)
          simp [Category.assoc, hov])
      ≫ (gmScalingP1_cover kbar).glueMorphisms (gmScalingP1_chart kbar)
          (gmScalingP1_chart_agreement kbar) =
    pullback.lift
        (pullback.lift ((Gm kbar).hom ≫ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left)
          (𝟙 (Gm kbar).left)
          (by
            have hov : (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left ≫
                (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom =
                (𝟙_ (Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)))).hom :=
              Over.w (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar)
            simp [Category.assoc, hov]))
        ((Gm kbar).hom ≫ r_1)
        (by rw [pullback.lift_fst, Category.assoc, h_r_1])
      ≫ gmScalingP1_chart kbar (1 : Fin 2) from ?_]
  · -- Step (f): the composed section ≫ chart-1 map is an open immersion
    -- (sub-task f helper).
    exact iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion r_1 h_r_1
  · -- Identify the LHS with `s ≫ (cover).f 1 ≫ glueMorphisms = s ≫ gmScalingP1_chart 1`.
    -- The inner pullback.lift `s ≫ (cover).f 1 = inner pullback.lift` by `pullback.lift_fst`
    -- of the outer pullback; then `(cover).f 1 ≫ glueMorphisms = gmScalingP1_chart 1`
    -- by `Cover.ι_glueMorphisms`.
    rw [Category.assoc, ← Scheme.Cover.ι_glueMorphisms (gmScalingP1_cover kbar)
          (gmScalingP1_chart kbar) (gmScalingP1_chart_agreement kbar) (1 : Fin 2),
        ← Category.assoc, pullback.lift_fst]

    ((Gm kbar).hom ≫ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left) ≫
        (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom =

private lemma iotaGm_isOpenImmersion [IsAlgClosed kbar] :
    IsOpenImmersion ((lift (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar)
        (𝟙 (Gm kbar))).left ≫ (gmScalingP1 kbar).left) := by
  -- Step (a): unfold `(lift _ _).left` to `pullback.lift _ _ _` via `Over.lift_left`,
  -- and expose `gmScalingP1.left` as its `glueMorphisms` form.
  rw [Over.lift_left]
  simp only [Over.comp_left, Over.id_left, Over.toUnit_left]
  change IsOpenImmersion (pullback.lift _ _ _ ≫
    (gmScalingP1_cover kbar).glueMorphisms (gmScalingP1_chart kbar)
      (gmScalingP1_chart_agreement kbar))
  -- Step (b): obtain the chart-1 factorisation `r_1` of `onePt.left`.
  obtain ⟨r_1, h_r_1⟩ := iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor kbar
  -- Step (c)+(d)+(e): the section `s` (named `iotaGm_chart1_section`) satisfies
  -- `s ≫ (cover).f 1 = inner pullback.lift` by `pullback.lift_fst`. Combined with
  -- `Cover.ι_glueMorphisms` for chart `1 : Fin 2`, this identifies the prefix
  -- composed with `glueMorphisms` to `s ≫ gmScalingP1_chart 1`.
  have hfact : pullback.lift
        ((Gm kbar).hom ≫ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left)
        (𝟙 (Gm kbar).left)
        (iotaGm_inner_lift_compat kbar)
      ≫ (gmScalingP1_cover kbar).glueMorphisms (gmScalingP1_chart kbar)
          (gmScalingP1_chart_agreement kbar) =
      iotaGm_chart1_section kbar r_1 h_r_1 ≫ gmScalingP1_chart kbar (1 : Fin 2) := by
    rw [← Scheme.Cover.ι_glueMorphisms (gmScalingP1_cover kbar)
          (gmScalingP1_chart kbar) (gmScalingP1_chart_agreement kbar) (1 : Fin 2),
      ← Category.assoc]
    congr 1
    -- `s ≫ (cover).f 1 = inner pullback.lift` by `pullback.lift_fst` of the outer pullback.
    exact pullback.lift_fst _ _ _
  rw [hfact]
  -- Step (f): the composed section ≫ chart-1 map is an open immersion (sub-task f helper).
  exact iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion r_1 h_r_1

11	/-!
12	# Typeclasses for `S`-schemes and `S`-morphisms
13	
14	We define these as thin wrappers around `CategoryTheory/Comma/OverClass`.
15	
16	## Main definition
17	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over`: `X.Over S` equips `X` with an `S`-scheme structure.
18	  `X ↘ S : X ⟶ S` is the structure morphism.
19	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.IsOver`: `f.IsOver S` asserts that `f` is an `S`-morphism.
20	
21	-/

33	/--
34	`X.Over S` is the typeclass containing the data of a structure morphism `X ↘ S : X ⟶ S`.
35	-/
36	protected abbrev Over (X S : Scheme.{u}) := OverClass X S
37	
38	/--
39	`X.CanonicallyOver S` is the typeclass containing the data of a structure morphism `X ↘ S : X ⟶ S`,
40	and that `S` is (uniquely) inferable from the structure of `X`.
41	-/
42	abbrev CanonicallyOver (X S : Scheme.{u}) := CanonicallyOverClass X S
43	
44	/-- Given `X.Over S` and `Y.Over S` and `f : X ⟶ Y`,
45	`f.IsOver S` is the typeclass asserting `f` commutes with the structure morphisms. -/
46	abbrev Hom.IsOver (f : X.Hom Y) (S : Scheme.{u}) [X.Over S] [Y.Over S] := HomIsOver f S
47	
48	@[simp]
49	lemma Hom.isOver_iff [X.Over S] [Y.Over S] {f : X ⟶ Y} : f.IsOver S ↔ f ≫ Y ↘ S = X ↘ S :=
50	  ⟨fun H ↦ H.1, fun h ↦ ⟨h⟩⟩
51	
52	/-! Also note the existence of `CategoryTheory.IsOverTower X Y S`. -/
53	
54	/-- Given `X.Over S`, this is the bundled object of `Over S`. -/
55	abbrev asOver (X S : Scheme.{u}) [X.Over S] := OverClass.asOver X S
56	
57	/-- Given a morphism `X ⟶ Y` with `f.IsOver S`, this is the bundled morphism in `Over S`. -/
58	abbrev Hom.asOver (f : X.Hom Y) (S : Scheme.{u}) [X.Over S] [Y.Over S] [f.IsOver S] :=
59	  OverClass.asOverHom S f
60	
61	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme

78	@[ext]
79	theorem OverMorphism.ext {X : T} {U V : Over X} {f g : U ⟶ V} (h : f.left = g.left) : f = g := by
80	  let ⟨_,b,_⟩ := f
81	  let ⟨_,e,_⟩ := g
82	  congr
83	  simp only [eq_iff_true_of_subsingleton]
84	
85	@[simp]
86	theorem over_right (U : Over X) : U.right = ⟨⟨⟩⟩ := by simp only
87	
88	@[simp]
89	theorem id_left (U : Over X) : Hom.left (𝟙 U) = 𝟙 U.left :=
90	  rfl
91	
92	@[simp, reassoc]
93	theorem comp_left (a b c : Over X) (f : a ⟶ b) (g : b ⟶ c) : (f ≫ g).left = f.left ≫ g.left :=
94	  rfl
95	
96	/-- To give an object in the over category, it suffices to give a morphism with codomain `X`. -/
97	@[simps! left hom]
98	def mk {X Y : T} (f : Y ⟶ X) : Over X :=
99	  CostructuredArrow.mk f
100	
101	/-- We can set up a coercion from arrows with codomain `X` to `over X`. This most likely should not
102	be a global instance, but it is sometimes useful. -/
103	@[instance_reducible]
104	def coeFromHom {X Y : T} : CoeOut (Y ⟶ X) (Over X) where coe := mk
105	
106	section
107	
108	attribute [local instance] coeFromHom

## iter-183 · parallel prover

When `OverClass.asOver` is annotated `@[simps! hom left]`, the generated
`asOver_hom : (asOver X S).hom = X ↘ S` simp lemma fires eagerly during
elaboration. This silently transforms `(asOver X S).hom` to `X ↘ S` in
many goals, breaking `rw [Over.w f]` and `rw [hfact]` patterns where
`hfact`'s LHS contains the original `(asOver X S).hom` form. The
displayed hypothesis still shows `.hom` but the elaborated form is
`X ↘ S`, so `rw` reports "Did not find pattern" even though the visual
forms match.

Workarounds discovered:
1. Term-mode `congrArg ((g ≫ ·)) (Over.w _)` bypasses the rewriter.
2. `aesop_cat` closes Over-axiom goals reliably.
3. `have := lemma; rwa [← hfact] at this` reverses the broken rewrite
   direction (rewriting goals via lemmas applied to terms, rather than
   into goal).

Consider documenting this trap explicitly in the lean4 skill — I spent
~10 turns debugging the `rw [Over.w]` failure before finding aesop_cat.
EOF

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

8	/-!
9	# Auslander–Buchsbaum formula (A.4.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.4.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring
14	⟹ Cohen–Macaulay" that the sibling sub-build **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
15	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective surface)
16	consumes.
17	
18	Per STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
19	startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the Albanese
20	geometry and lives entirely under `RingTheory.*`. Mathlib at the project's
21	pinned commit (`b80f227`) exposes `IsRegularLocalRing` and the categorical
22	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on an abelian category (specialised to
23	`ModuleCat R`), as well as the regular-sequence definition
24	`RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`. The depth function `Module.depth`, the
25	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula, and the Cohen–Macaulay predicate are *not* in
26	Mathlib at the pinned commit — they are the new content scaffolded here.
27	
28	## Status (iter-178 update — projectiveDimension closed)
29	
30	This file was originally landed iter-175 (Lane F file-skeleton) with each
31	blueprint-pinned declaration carrying the *intended* substantive type
32	signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
33	`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex`). The bodies were
34	scheduled for iter-176+ work; the chapter is independent of the rest of
35	Route A's geometric infrastructure, making it a good parallel-work
36	candidate.
37	
38	iter-178 closed `Module.projectiveDimension` kernel-clean as a one-liner
39	re-export of the categorical `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension`. The
40	remaining five typed `sorry` bodies (`depth`, `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`,
41	`depth_of_short_exact`, `auslander_buchsbaum_formula`,
42	`CohenMacaulay.of_regular`) are substantive multi-iter content and stay
43	gated on dedicated body lanes.
44	
45	The 7 pinned declarations are:
46	
47	1. `RingTheory.Module.depth` (noncomputable def, ~6 LOC) — the `I`-depth of a
48	   finite `R`-module as the supremum of lengths of `M`-regular sequences in
49	   `I`, valued in `ℕ∞`.
50	2. `RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — for
51	   `(R, 𝔪)` Noetherian local and `M ≠ 0` finite, `depth(M)` equals the
52	   smallest `i` with `Ext^i_R(κ, M) ≠ 0`. Encoded via the depth-bound iff
53	   `Ext` vanishes below.
54	3. `Module.projectiveDimension` (noncomputable def, ~3 LOC) — an
55	   `R`-module-side wrapper for the categorical
56	   `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R M)`. The categorical
57	   version exists in Mathlib `b80f227`; this is the re-export pinned by the
58	   blueprint.
59	4. `RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact` (theorem, ~12 LOC) — the
60	   depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequalities (Stacks 00LE).
61	5. `RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the formula
62	   `pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)` for a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite
63	   projective dimension over a Noetherian local ring.
64	6. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay` (class, ~3 LOC) — `IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing R`
65	   encoded as `depth(R) = krullDim R`.
66	7. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular` (theorem, ~6 LOC) — a regular local
67	   ring is Cohen–Macaulay (the consumer-facing input for A.4.a).
68	
69	## Note on type expressivity
70	
71	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
72	declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
73	
74	- `depth` returns `ℕ∞` and is defined by the regular-sequence supremum.
75	- `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` is encoded as the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`
76	  vanishing characterisation (the smallest non-vanishing `Ext` index = depth).
77	- `projectiveDimension` re-exports the categorical
78	  `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on `ModuleCat.of R M`.
79	- `depth_of_short_exact` packages the three Stacks 00LE inequalities into a
80	  conjunction.
81	- `auslander_buchsbaum_formula` is the numeric equation
82	  `pd + depth = depth(R)`.
83	- `CohenMacaulay` is the equation `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`.
84	- `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` is an `IsRegularLocalRing → IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing`
85	  implication, the consumer-facing statement.
86	
87	Unfolding any declaration exposes the named substantive content (a regular-
88	sequence supremum, an `Ext`-vanishing characterisation, the categorical
89	projective dimension, …); no `Iso.refl _` / `Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` /
90	empty-content `proof_wanted` placeholders are used.
91	
92	## References
93	
94	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex` (~560 LOC,
95	7 pins). Stacks Project tags 00LF (definition-depth), 00LP (lemma-depth-ext),
96	00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses), 090V (proposition-Auslander–Buchsbaum), 00N4
97	(definition-local-ring-CM), 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM). Matsumura,
98	*Commutative Ring Theory*, Theorem 19.1. Auslander–Buchsbaum, "Homological
99	dimension in local rings", 1957.

112	/-! ## §1. Depth of a finite module
113	
114	The `I`-depth of a finite `R`-module `M` is the supremum in
115	`{0, 1, 2, …, ∞}` of the lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in `I`
116	(provided `IM ≠ M`; if `IM = M` we set `depth_I(M) = ∞`). Mathlib `b80f227`
117	exposes the regular-sequence predicate `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`
118	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.RegularSequence`) but not the resulting
119	numeric depth function — that is the gap this declaration fills.
120	
121	Blueprint reference: `def:depth` (Stacks tag 00LF). -/
122	
123	/-- The **`I`-depth** of a finite `R`-module `M`: the supremum (in `ℕ∞`) of
124	lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in the ideal `I`.
125	
126	When `IM = M` (the "trivial-quotient" case, e.g. `M = 0` or `I = R`) the
127	supremum is taken to be `⊤` by convention. When `(R, 𝔪)` is local one usually
128	calls `depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M` simply *the depth* of `M`.
129	
130	iter-176+: the body is the supremum
131	```
132	sSup { (n : ℕ∞) | ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = n ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ I) ∧
133	                  RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs }
134	```
135	folded with the `IM = M` clause. The signature is non-tautological: it
136	asserts a function `(Ideal R) → (M : Type v) → ℕ∞` matching the Stacks
137	00LF definition.
138	
139	**iter-179 Mathlib-gap check (Lane F Target 2 STRETCH)**: a pinned-commit
140	audit of `Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.Depth` (the only file under
141	`Mathlib/RingTheory/*` containing the word "depth") confirms that Mathlib
142	`b80f227` ships only depth-zero lemmas (`IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff`)
143	and *not* the numeric depth function itself. The one-liner re-export route
144	is therefore not available; the body stays a typed `sorry` until an
145	iter-180+ body lane fills the supremum-with-`IM=M` clause directly. -/
146	noncomputable def depth {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (_I : Ideal R)
147	    (_M : Type v) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : ℕ∞ :=
148	  open Classical in
149	  if _I • (⊤ : Submodule R _M) = ⊤ then (⊤ : ℕ∞)
150	  else sSup { n : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = n ∧
151	    (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ _I) ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular _M rs }
152	
153	end Module
154	
155	end RingTheory
156	
157	/-! ## §2. Projective dimension
158	
159	Mathlib `b80f227` exposes the categorical
160	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension : C → WithBot ℕ∞` on an abelian category
161	with enough projectives (file `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Projective.Dimension`).
162	For `R`-modules this specialises to `ModuleCat.of R M`. The blueprint pins the
163	`Module.projectiveDimension` name as the re-export that downstream consumers
164	can use directly on an `R`-module without first packaging it in `ModuleCat`.
165	
166	Blueprint reference: `def:projective_dimension`. -/
167	
168	namespace Module
169	
170	/-- The **projective dimension** of an `R`-module `M`, defined as the
171	categorical projective dimension of `ModuleCat.of R M`.
172	
173	This is a re-export of `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` specialised to
174	the abelian category `ModuleCat R`. The categorical definition is the
175	infimum (in `WithBot ℕ∞`) of `n : ℕ` such that all `Ext^i(M, -)` vanish for
176	`i > n`, equivalently the smallest length of a projective resolution of `M`.
177	
178	Mathlib has the categorical `projectiveDimension` and the module-specific
179	`ModuleCat.projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv`; the blueprint pins the
180	wrapper name so downstream consumers can write
181	`Module.projectiveDimension R M` rather than threading `ModuleCat.of`.
182	
183	The body is the one-line re-export
184	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)`; iter-178 closed
185	this declaration kernel-clean. -/
186	noncomputable def projectiveDimension (R : Type u) [Ring R]
187	    (_M : Type u) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : WithBot ℕ∞ :=
188	  CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)
189	
190	end Module
191	
192	namespace RingTheory

196	/-! ## §3. Depth via Ext characterisation
197	
198	For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` with residue field `κ = R/𝔪` and a
199	nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the depth of `M` equals the smallest index `i`
200	at which `Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is nonzero (Stacks tag 00LP).
201	
202	This is the lemma A.4.a's downstream consumer ultimately reads off:
203	combined with the regular-sequence definition (`depth`), the Ext
204	characterisation provides both the *lower bound* (regular sequences exhibit
205	`Ext^i = 0` for `i < length(rs)`) and the *upper bound* (failure of any
206	extension lifts a non-zero element in `Ext^{depth}(κ, M)`).
207	
208	The signature pins the equivalence via the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`-vanishing-
209	below: `n ≤ depth(M) ↔ Ext^i(κ, M) = 0 for all i < n`. This is logically
210	equivalent to "depth(M) = smallest i with Ext^i ≠ 0" and is the form most
211	convenient for inductive proofs (Stacks 00LP proof: pick `x ∈ 𝔪`
212	non-zero-divisor, use long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)` on `0 → M → M → M/xM → 0`).
213	
214	Blueprint reference: `lem:depth_via_ext` (Stacks tag 00LP). -/
215	
216	/-- **Depth via Ext characterisation.** For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`
217	with residue field `κ = R/𝔪` and a nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the
218	depth-bound `n ≤ depth(M)` is equivalent to the vanishing of `Ext^i_R(κ, M)`
219	for all `i < n`. Equivalently, `depth(M)` is the smallest `i` at which
220	`Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is nonzero.
221	
222	iter-176+: the body proceeds by induction on `n` via the long exact sequence
223	of `Ext^*(κ, -)` applied to `0 → M → M → M/xM → 0` for a non-zero-divisor
224	`x ∈ 𝔪`. The base case `n = 0` is `Hom(κ, M) ≠ 0 ↔ depth(M) = 0`, which is
225	the standard "the maximal ideal contains a zero-divisor on `M` iff
226	`𝔪 ∈ Ass(M)`" characterisation (Stacks 00LC). For the iter-175 file-skeleton
227	the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
228	theorem depth_eq_smallest_ext_index
229	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
230	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
231	    [Nontrivial M] (n : ℕ) :
232	    (n : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M ↔
233	      ∀ i : ℕ, i < n →
234	        ∀ e : Abelian.Ext.{u}
235	            (ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R))
236	            (ModuleCat.of R M) i, e = 0 := by
237	  sorry
238	
239	/-! ## §4. Depth on a short exact sequence
240	
241	For a short exact sequence `0 → N' → N → N'' → 0` of nonzero finite modules
242	over a Noetherian local ring, the three modules' depths satisfy three
243	crosswise inequalities (Stacks tag 00LE), each a direct read-off of the
244	long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)` sequence and the depth-via-Ext characterisation
245	of §3.
246	
247	Blueprint reference: `lem:depth_short_exact_sequence` (Stacks tag 00LE). -/
248	
249	/-! ### Helper A (iter-182 Lane G, axiom-clean): Ext-vanishing from strict depth bound
250	
251	For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` and a nonzero finite `R`-module `M`,
252	if `(i : ℕ∞) < depth M` then every element of `Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is zero.
253	
254	This packages `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` for the LES chase: the
255	`n ≤ depth M` form with `n := i + 1` instantiates the `∀ j < i + 1`
256	quantifier at `j = i`. Body is kernel-clean modulo the typed sorry of
257	`depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`. -/
258	private lemma ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth
259	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
260	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
261	    [Nontrivial M] {i : ℕ}
262	    (h : (i : ℕ∞) < depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M)
263	    (e : CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.{u}
264	        (ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R))
265	        (ModuleCat.of R M) i) : e = 0 := by
266	  have h' : ((i + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M := by
267	    have hcast : ((i + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) = (i : ℕ∞) + 1 := by push_cast; ring
268	    rw [hcast]; exact Order.add_one_le_of_lt h
269	  exact (depth_eq_smallest_ext_index (M := M) (i + 1)).mp h' i (Nat.lt_succ_self i) e
270	
271	/-! ### Helper B (iter-182 Lane G, axiom-clean): `ℕ∞` tsub bridge
272	
273	If `(a : ℕ) ≤ d - 1` in `ℕ∞` and `1 ≤ a` (in `ℕ`), then
274	`((a + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) ≤ d`.
275	
276	Case-split on `d = ⊤` (trivial) and `d = ↑n` (drop to `ℕ` arithmetic).
277	Used for the `depth N' - 1` shift in the second SES inequality. -/
278	private lemma natCast_add_one_le_of_le_sub_one
279	    {d : ℕ∞} {a : ℕ} (ha : 1 ≤ a) (h : (a : ℕ∞) ≤ d - 1) :
280	    ((a + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) ≤ d := by
281	  rcases eq_or_ne d ⊤ with hd | hd
282	  · simp [hd]
283	  · obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ := WithTop.ne_top_iff_exists.mp hd
284	    -- Reduce to ℕ: turn `↑a ≤ ↑n - 1` into `a ≤ n - 1`, then `a + 1 ≤ n`.
285	    have h₂ : (a : ℕ∞) ≤ ((n - 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) := by
286	      refine h.trans (le_of_eq ?_)
287	      rcases n with _ | n
288	      · rfl
289	      · push_cast; rfl
290	    have han : a ≤ n - 1 := by exact_mod_cast h₂
291	    have hle : a + 1 ≤ n := by omega
292	    exact Nat.cast_le.mpr hle
293	
294	theorem depth_of_short_exact
295	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
296	    {N' N N'' : Type u}
297	    [AddCommGroup N'] [Module R N'] [_root_.Module.Finite R N'] [Nontrivial N']
298	    [AddCommGroup N] [Module R N] [_root_.Module.Finite R N] [Nontrivial N]
299	    [AddCommGroup N''] [Module R N''] [_root_.Module.Finite R N''] [Nontrivial N'']
300	    (f : N' →ₗ[R] N) (g : N →ₗ[R] N'')
301	    (_hf : Function.Injective f) (_hg : Function.Surjective g)
302	    (_hex : Function.Exact f g) :
303	    min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N')
304	        (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'')
305	      ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N
306	    ∧ min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N)
307	          (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' - 1)
308	        ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N''
309	    ∧ min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N)
310	          (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'' + 1)
311	        ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' := by
312	  -- Package the SES as a `ShortComplex.ShortExact` in `ModuleCat.{u} R`.
313	  let S : ShortComplex (ModuleCat.{u} R) :=
314	    ShortComplex.mk (ModuleCat.ofHom f) (ModuleCat.ofHom g)
315	      (by ext x; simpa using _hex.apply_apply_eq_zero x)
316	  have hS : S.ShortExact :=
317	    ModuleCat.shortComplex_shortExact S _hex _hf _hg
318	  -- The residue field as a ModuleCat object.
319	  set κ : ModuleCat.{u} R := ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R) with hκ
320	  refine ⟨?_, ?_, ?_⟩
321	  · -- (1) min(depth N', depth N'') ≤ depth N
322	    rw [← ENat.forall_natCast_le_iff_le]
323	    intro a ha
324	    rw [le_min_iff] at ha
325	    obtain ⟨haN', haN''⟩ := ha
326	    rw [depth_eq_smallest_ext_index]
327	    intro i hi e
328	    -- `e : Ext κ S.X₂ i = Ext κ (of R N) i`; goal `e = 0`.
329	    have hicast : (i : ℕ∞) < (a : ℕ∞) := by exact_mod_cast hi
330	    have hiN' : (i : ℕ∞) < depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' := hicast.trans_le haN'
331	    have hiN'' : (i : ℕ∞) < depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'' := hicast.trans_le haN''
332	    -- `e ∘ S.g ∈ Ext κ (of R N'') i = 0`.
333	    have heg : e.comp (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g) (add_zero i) = 0 :=
334	      ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth hiN'' _
335	    obtain ⟨x₁, hx₁⟩ :=
336	      CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₂ κ hS e heg
337	    -- `x₁ ∈ Ext κ (of R N') i = 0`.
338	    have hx₁_zero : x₁ = 0 := ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth hiN' _
339	    rw [hx₁_zero] at hx₁
340	    simpa using hx₁.symm
341	  · -- (2) min(depth N, depth N' - 1) ≤ depth N''
342	    rw [← ENat.forall_natCast_le_iff_le]
343	    intro a ha
344	    rw [le_min_iff] at ha
345	    obtain ⟨haN, haN'sub⟩ := ha
346	    rw [depth_eq_smallest_ext_index]
347	    intro i hi e
348	    -- `e : Ext κ S.X₃ i = Ext κ (of R N'') i`; goal `e = 0`.
349	    have hicast : (i : ℕ∞) < (a : ℕ∞) := by exact_mod_cast hi
350	    have hiN : (i : ℕ∞) < depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N := hicast.trans_le haN
351	    -- `↑(i+1) < depth N'`: use Helper B with `a` and the inequality `hi : i + 1 ≤ a`.
352	    have hia : 1 ≤ a := by omega
353	    have ha1 : ((a + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' :=
354	      natCast_add_one_le_of_le_sub_one hia haN'sub
355	    have hsucc : ((i + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) < depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' := by
356	      have : ((i + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) < ((a + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) := by exact_mod_cast Nat.add_lt_add_right hi 1
357	      exact this.trans_le ha1
358	    -- `e ∘ extClass ∈ Ext κ (of R N') (i + 1) = 0`.
359	    have hext : e.comp hS.extClass rfl = 0 :=
360	      ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth hsucc _
361	    obtain ⟨x₂, hx₂⟩ :=
362	      CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₃ κ hS e rfl hext
363	    -- `x₂ ∈ Ext κ (of R N) i = 0`.
364	    have hx₂_zero : x₂ = 0 := ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth hiN _
365	    rw [hx₂_zero] at hx₂
366	    simpa using hx₂.symm
367	  · -- (3) min(depth N, depth N'' + 1) ≤ depth N'
368	    rw [← ENat.forall_natCast_le_iff_le]
369	    intro a ha
370	    rw [le_min_iff] at ha
371	    obtain ⟨haN, haN''add⟩ := ha
372	    rw [depth_eq_smallest_ext_index]
373	    intro i hi e
374	    -- `e : Ext κ S.X₁ i = Ext κ (of R N') i`; goal `e = 0`.
375	    have hicast : (i : ℕ∞) < (a : ℕ∞) := by exact_mod_cast hi
376	    have hiN : (i : ℕ∞) < depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N := hicast.trans_le haN
377	    -- `e ∘ S.f ∈ Ext κ (of R N) i = 0`.
378	    have hef : e.comp (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero i) = 0 :=
379	      ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth hiN _
380	    -- Split on `i = 0` vs `i ≥ 1`. For `i ≥ 1`, use `covariant_sequence_exact₁`.
381	    -- For `i = 0`, postcomposition by `S.f` is injective (since `S.f` is mono).
382	    rcases Nat.eq_zero_or_pos i with hi0 | hi0
383	    · subst hi0
384	      -- `e : Ext κ S.X₁ 0`; postcomp by `S.f` is injective; image is `e ∘ S.f = 0`,
385	      -- so `e = 0`.
386	      have hmono : CategoryTheory.Mono S.f :=
387	        (ModuleCat.mono_iff_injective _).mpr _hf
388	      have hinj := CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.postcomp_mk₀_injective_of_mono κ S.f
389	      apply hinj
390	      simpa using hef
391	    · -- `i ≥ 1`. Let `i = j + 1` and use `covariant_sequence_exact₁` at
392	      -- `n₀ = j, n₁ = i = j + 1`.
393	      obtain ⟨j, rfl⟩ := Nat.exists_eq_succ_of_ne_zero (Nat.pos_iff_ne_zero.mp hi0)
394	      -- `e : Ext κ (of R N') (j + 1)`. We need `Ext κ (of R N'') j = 0`.
395	      -- From `↑(j+2) ≤ ↑a ≤ depth N'' + 1`, get `↑j + 1 ≤ depth N''`, so `↑j < depth N''`.
396	      have hjN'' : (j : ℕ∞) < depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'' := by
397	        have hja : j + 2 ≤ a := by omega
398	        have h_j2 : ((j + 2 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'' + 1 := by
399	          refine le_trans ?_ haN''add
400	          exact_mod_cast hja
401	        have hcast : ((j + 2 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) = ((j + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) + 1 := by push_cast; ring
402	        rw [hcast] at h_j2
403	        have h_canc : ((j + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'' :=
404	          (ENat.add_le_add_iff_right (by norm_num : (1 : ℕ∞) ≠ ⊤)).mp h_j2
405	        have hcast2 : ((j + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) = (j : ℕ∞) + 1 := by push_cast; ring
406	        rw [hcast2] at h_canc
407	        exact (ENat.add_one_le_iff (by simp : (j : ℕ∞) ≠ ⊤)).mp h_canc
408	      obtain ⟨x₃, hx₃⟩ :=
409	        CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁ κ hS e hef rfl
410	      -- `x₃ ∈ Ext κ (of R N'') j = 0`.
411	      have hx₃_zero : x₃ = 0 := ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth hjN'' _
412	      rw [hx₃_zero] at hx₃
413	      simpa using hx₃.symm
414	
415	end Module

417	/-! ## §5. The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula
418	
419	For a nonzero finite module `M` of finite projective dimension over a
420	Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, the **Auslander–Buchsbaum formula** reads
421	```
422	  pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)
423	```
424	(Stacks tag 090V). The proof inducts on `depth(M)`: the base case
425	`depth(M) = 0` uses a minimal finite free resolution of `M` and the
426	"what is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF) plus iterated application of the
427	depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma (§4) to bound `depth(R)` against the
428	resolution length; the inductive step picks a common non-zero-divisor
429	`x ∈ 𝔪` on both `R` and `M`, applies the snake lemma to obtain a minimal
430	finite free resolution of `M/xM` over `R/xR` of the same length, and uses
431	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`.
432	
433	Blueprint reference: `thm:auslander_buchsbaum` (Stacks tag 090V). -/
434	
435	/-- **The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula.** Let `(R, 𝔪)` be a Noetherian local
436	ring and let `M` be a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite projective
437	dimension. Then
438	```
439	  pd_R(M) + depth_R(M) = depth(R).
440	```
441	
442	The hypothesis "finite projective dimension" is encoded by an explicit
443	upper bound `n : ℕ` on the projective dimension (so the formula compares
444	finite numeric quantities cleanly without `WithBot ℕ∞`-arithmetic
445	subtleties).
446	
447	iter-176+: the body is the induction on `depth(M)` outlined in the
448	chapter — base case via the minimal-finite-free-resolution +
449	"what is exact" + iterated `Module.depth_of_short_exact`; inductive step
450	via the snake lemma on multiplication by a common non-zero-divisor and
451	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`. For the iter-175
452	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
453	theorem auslander_buchsbaum_formula
454	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
455	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
456	    [Nontrivial M]
457	    (n : ℕ)
458	    (_hpd : _root_.Module.projectiveDimension R M = (n : WithBot ℕ∞)) :
459	    (n : ℕ∞) + Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M
460	      = Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R := by
461	  sorry

463	/-! ## §6. Cohen–Macaulay local rings
464	
465	A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth equals
466	its Krull dimension (Stacks tag 00N4). Mathlib `b80f227` has neither the
467	predicate nor the class — this file is the upstream gap-fill.
468	
469	Blueprint reference: `def:cohen_macaulay_local` (Stacks tag 00N4). -/
470	
471	/-- A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth
472	equals its Krull dimension: `depth(R) = dim R`.
473	
474	Encoded as a `Prop`-valued type class so downstream consumers can write
475	`[CohenMacaulay R]` and use Cohen–Macaulay as a hypothesis. Mathlib at the
476	pinned commit (`b80f227`) does not expose any Cohen–Macaulay predicate;
477	this is the upstream gap-fill.
478	
479	iter-176+: the predicate is `Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R =
480	ringKrullDim R`. For the iter-175 file-skeleton the carrier definition is a
481	typed `sorry` at the `Prop` level — substantively, the predicate is the
482	named equality, but we package it as a `class` so use sites are uniform. -/
483	class CohenMacaulay (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
484	    [IsNoetherianRing R] : Prop where
485	  /-- The Cohen–Macaulay equation: `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`. The numeric
486	  comparison is in `WithBot ℕ∞` after coercion of the `ℕ∞`-valued depth. -/
487	  depth_eq_krullDim :
488	    (Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R : WithBot ℕ∞) = ringKrullDim R
489	
490	/-! ## §7. Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay
491	
492	The consumer-facing input for A.4.a: every regular Noetherian local ring is
493	Cohen–Macaulay (Stacks tag 00OD). The direct proof: pick a minimal
494	generating set `x_1, …, x_d` of `𝔪` (where `d = dim R`), use that `R` is a
495	domain (Stacks 00NQ) to start an `R`-regular sequence, and induct on
496	dimension — each `R/(x_1, …, x_c)` is again regular of dimension `d - c`,
497	so `x_1, …, x_d` is an `R`-regular sequence and `depth(R) ≥ d`. The reverse
498	inequality `depth(R) ≤ dim R` is the standard depth bound (Stacks 00LK).
499	
500	Blueprint reference: `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` (Stacks tag 00OD). -/
501	
502	namespace CohenMacaulay
503	
504	/-! ### Helper 1 (axiom-clean): length-bound on regular sequences
505	
506	For a Noetherian local ring `R`, every `R`-regular sequence has length at most
507	`ringKrullDim R`. This is the **upper bound** half of Stacks 00OD: it is the
508	specialisation of the equality
509	`ringKrullDim (R / ofList rs) + rs.length = ringKrullDim R`
510	(`ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular`) to the observation that
511	`ringKrullDim (R / ofList rs) ≥ 0` whenever the quotient is nontrivial, which it
512	is precisely because `IsRegular` rules out `rs • ⊤ = ⊤`.
513	
514	iter-181 Lane G: closed kernel-clean. -/
515	private lemma length_le_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular
516	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
517	    {rs : List R} (h : RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs) :
518	    (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞) ≤ ringKrullDim R := by
519	  have heq := ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular rs h
520	  have hntq : Nontrivial (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) := by
521	    rw [Ideal.Quotient.nontrivial_iff]
522	    intro habs
523	    apply h.top_ne_smul
524	    change (⊤ : Submodule R R) = (Ideal.ofList rs) • ⊤
525	    rw [habs]; simp
526	  have hnn : (0 : WithBot ℕ∞) ≤ ringKrullDim (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) :=
527	    ringKrullDim_nonneg_of_nontrivial
528	  calc (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞)
529	      = 0 + (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞) := by simp
530	    _ ≤ ringKrullDim (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) + (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞) := by gcongr
531	    _ = ringKrullDim R := heq
532	
533	/-! ### Helper 2 (substantive typed `sorry`): system-of-parameters as a regular sequence
534	
535	For a regular local ring `R` of Krull dimension `d = (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank`,
536	a minimal generating set `x_1, …, x_d` of the maximal ideal `𝔪` is an `R`-regular
537	sequence. This is the **lower bound** half of Stacks 00OD.
538	
539	The proof in Stacks 00OD uses:
540	1. A regular local ring is an integral domain (Stacks 00NQ).
541	2. For each `c < d`, the quotient `R / (x_1, …, x_c)` is again a regular local
542	   ring, of Krull dimension `d - c` (Krull's principal ideal theorem +
543	   `lemma-one-equation`).
544	
545	Step (1) — `IsRegularLocalRing R ⟹ IsDomain R` — is **not present in Mathlib at
546	the pinned commit** (`b80f227`); a `lean_leansearch` for "regular local ring is
547	a domain" returns nothing relevant. Step (2) requires the regular-quotient
548	inductive structure (Stacks 00NQ + height-one quotient regularity), which is
549	similarly absent. Both are substantive multi-iter content.
550	
551	The signature here is the non-tautological existence statement: a list `rs : List R`
552	with `rs.length = (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank`, `rs ⊆ maximalIdeal R`, and
553	`IsRegular R rs`. Downstream, `of_regular` consumes this directly to close the
554	`depth ≥ d` lower bound.
555	
556	iter-182+: discharge candidates are (a) formalising `IsRegularLocalRing ⟹ IsDomain`
557	first (the Stacks 00NQ argument: a regular local ring has no embedded prime in
558	the zero ideal, hence the zero ideal is prime); (b) leveraging an alternative
559	characterisation of depth that bypasses the explicit regular-sequence
560	construction (e.g.\ depth via `Ext` over the residue field, Stacks 00LP, which
561	is its own lane `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`). -/
562	lemma exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal
563	    (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
564	    [IsRegularLocalRing R] :
565	    ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank
566	        ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
567	        ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs := by
568	  sorry
569	
570	/-- **Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay.** Every regular Noetherian
571	local ring is Cohen–Macaulay: a minimal generating set of `𝔪` is an
572	`R`-regular sequence of length `dim R`, so `depth(R) ≥ dim R`; combined
573	with the standard upper bound `depth(R) ≤ dim R` (Stacks 00LK) this gives
574	`depth(R) = dim R`.
575	
576	This is the consumer-facing input for **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
577	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective
578	surface): the local ring `O_{S,x}` of a regular projective surface at a
579	closed point is regular of Krull dimension `2`, hence Cohen–Macaulay,
580	hence has depth `2`, which is exactly the input the local-cohomology
581	vanishing `H^i_x(O_S) = 0` for `i < 2` needs (Stacks 0AVF; see Hartshorne
582	III.7).
583	
584	iter-181 Lane G **STRUCTURAL**: body is now decomposed into two
585	typed helper lemmas — `length_le_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular` (the upper
586	bound, closed kernel-clean from
587	`ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular`) and
588	`exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (the lower bound, typed `sorry` on the
589	Mathlib gap `IsRegularLocalRing ⟹ IsDomain` + regular-quotient induction).
590	The combined assembly into `depth = ringKrullDim` is closed inline below,
591	so the only residual `sorry` in this `instance` body is the named helper. -/
592	instance of_regular (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
593	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R] : CohenMacaulay R where
594	  depth_eq_krullDim := by
595	    -- Step 1: simplify `Module.depth` via the `else` branch
596	    --   (since `𝔪 • ⊤ = 𝔪 ≠ ⊤` for a local ring's maximal ideal).
597	    have h𝔪 : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (⊤ : Submodule R R)
598	        ≠ (⊤ : Submodule R R) := by
599	      have heq : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (⊤ : Submodule R R)
600	          = IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by simp
601	      rw [heq]
602	      exact (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal.isMaximal R).ne_top
603	    rw [Module.depth, if_neg h𝔪]
604	    -- Step 2: convert RHS to the spanFinrank using
605	    -- `IsRegularLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal`.
606	    rw [← IsRegularLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal]
607	    -- Goal: ((sSup {n | …} : ℕ∞) : WithBot ℕ∞)
608	    --         = ((spanFinrank 𝔪 : ℕ) : WithBot ℕ∞)
609	    -- Step 3: it suffices to show the sSup equals spanFinrank as ℕ∞,
610	    -- via antisymmetry: upper bound from Helper 1, lower bound from Helper 2.
611	    have h1 : (sSup { n : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = n ∧
612	        (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
613	          ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs }
614	        : ℕ∞) = ((IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank : ℕ∞) := by
615	      apply le_antisymm
616	      · -- Upper bound: every element of the sSup-set is at most spanFinrank.
617	        apply sSup_le
618	        rintro n ⟨rs, rfl, _, hreg⟩
619	        have hub := length_le_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular hreg
620	        rw [← IsRegularLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal] at hub
621	        exact_mod_cast hub
622	      · -- Lower bound: spanFinrank is achieved by Helper 2's regular sequence.
623	        obtain ⟨rs, hlen, hmem, hreg⟩ := exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal R
624	        apply le_sSup
625	        refine ⟨rs, ?_, hmem, hreg⟩
626	        exact_mod_cast hlen
627	    rw [h1]
628	    -- Final coercion: `((n : ℕ∞) : WithBot ℕ∞) = ((n : ℕ) : WithBot ℕ∞)`
629	    -- is the standard `Nat.cast`-tower commutation.
630	    rfl
631	
632	end CohenMacaulay
633	
634	end RingTheory

15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit
