3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

67	### Primary target this iter: `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` (L228)
68	
69	The substantive inductive chase per Stacks 00LE. The Stacks proof:
70	inducts on `depth M` using the SES `0 → M →[x] M → M/xM → 0` where
71	`x` is a regular element. Two cases:
72	- `depth M = 0`: there's a non-zero `Ext^0(κ, M) = Hom(κ, M)` element.
73	- `depth M = d+1`: there's a regular element `x`, so `depth (M/xM) = d`,
74	  and the LES of `Ext^*(κ, ·)` gives the recursive identity.
75	
76	ETA ~100-150 LOC. Reuse iter-182's `ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth`
77	helper (Tier-2 modulo `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` itself — so this
78	target is itself the upstream! The Tier-2 inheritance flips when this

85	splitting argument). The Tier-2 helper `depth_of_short_exact` from

3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ of a Weil divisor (RR.2$_\ast$)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of $\mathscr L(D)$; Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf $\mathcal O_X(D)$ from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
29	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \Div(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor $D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]$ to its associated invertible
39	sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ on $C$. The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	$\chi$-identity proof of \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor $D$ via the
51	``$D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]$'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$, which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much $\chi$-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}) constructs
70	the line bundle $\mathcal O_C([P])$ associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	$P \in C$ directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	\Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting $\mathcal O_C(D)$ at $D = [P]$ as canonically
74	isomorphic to \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via the
77	$\chi$-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of $C$ both reference $\mathcal O_C(D)$ for general $D$ (not
84	just $D = [P]$): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	$D \in \Div(C)$ and reads off $\deg(D)$ from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	\Cref{def:sheafOf}).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
92	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group $\Div(C)$ is the one defined in
103	\Cref{def:codim1_cycles} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is \Cref{def:prime_divisor}; the order
105	$\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor $Q$ is \Cref{def:order_at_point}; the
106	principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a non-zero rational function is
107	\Cref{def:principal_divisor}; the closed-point divisor $[P]$ is
108	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}, and the present chapter's
111	\Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} makes the comparison
112	$\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}$ explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation $\mathcal O_C([P])$:
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on $\mathbf{SheafOfModules}$ (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData $\to$ SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no $\mathbf{SheafOfModules}$ dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-$\mathcal K_C$} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf $D$} as well, and \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} verifies
127	that the general construction at $D = [P]$ recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-$\mathcal K_C$
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	$\mathscr L(D)$) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general $D$ and at the $D = [P]$
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	$\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}$ a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.
135	
136	\section{The invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor, def:order_at_point,
143	        def:principal_divisor}
144	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
145	  % sheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
146	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
147	  % (Cartier $\leftrightarrow$ Weil correspondence on an integral
148	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of $\mathscr L(D)$ as a
149	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field $K$). Read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
151	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
152	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
153	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
154	  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
155	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
156	  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
157	  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
158	  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, so $f_i^{-1}$ and
159	  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
160	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
161	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If $X$ is an integral
162	  % scheme, the homomorphism $\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X$ of
163	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
164	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of $\mathscr K$, which
165	  % in this case is the constant sheaf $K$, where $K$ is the function
166	  % field of $X$."
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
168	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on $X$ and
169	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes $Y$, i.e., subschemes
170	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
171	  % Note also that if $X$ is an integral separated noetherian locally
172	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
173	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
174	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
175	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
176	  $\mathscr L(D)$), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-$K$
177	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
178	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
179	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
180	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
181	  $\bar k$ and let $D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor
182	  on $C$, with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors $Q$ of $C$ and
183	  supported on a finite set $\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}$
184	  (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}). The \emph{invertible sheaf
185	  $\mathcal O_C(D)$ associated to $D$}, also written
186	  $\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)$ in Hartshorne's notation, is the
187	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the constant sheaf
188	  $\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)$ (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
189	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
190	  field) defined section-wise by
191	  \[
192	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
193	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
194	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
195	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
196	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
197	  \]
198	  for each open $U \subseteq C$. Restriction along $V \hookrightarrow U$
199	  is the identity on the underlying carrier $K(C)$, which preserves
200	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
201	  the constraint set on $U$ embeds into the constraint set on $V$). The
202	  $\bar k$-module structure on
203	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ is restricted from the
204	  $\bar k$-module structure on $K(C)$ (a $\bar k$-scalar multiple of a
205	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
206	
207	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
208	  formal sum $D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]$ packages, on a curve where prime
209	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
210	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness $+$ dimension~$1$), the Cartier-divisor
211	  data $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
212	  open $U_i$ containing exactly one prime divisor $Q$ in the support of
213	  $D$, pick a uniformiser $\pi_Q \in K(C)$ at $Q$ and set
214	  $f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}$; on opens disjoint from the support of $D$, set
215	  $f_i := 1$. The transitions on overlaps $U_i \cap U_j$ are units in
216	  $\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}$ for each prime $Q$ in the overlap (any
217	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
218	  $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
219	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original $D$. The
220	  ``$f_i^{-1}$-generated'' subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$ from Hartshorne's
221	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
222	  the local generator $f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}$ has order $-n_Q$ at $Q$,
223	  so the local sections are exactly $f \in K(C)$ with
224	  $\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0$, i.e.\ $\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q$, on
225	  $U_i$.
226	
227	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
228	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} of \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP})
229	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
230	  \mathcal K_C$ is an invertible sheaf on $C$, independently of the
231	  choice of uniformisers $\pi_Q$ (any two choices differ by a unit, so
232	  they generate the same $\mathcal O_C$-submodule).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
235	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
236	  divisor $D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}$ and returns a
237	  $\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$ on
238	  $C.\text{left.toTopCat}$, where
239	  $J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}$ is the
240	  standard topology on the topological space underlying $C$. The
241	  closed-point specialisation $\mathcal O_C([P])$ is
242	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} of
243	  \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}; their agreement at $D = [P]$ is
244	  \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} below. The construction follows
245	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
246	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
247	  $\bar k$-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
248	  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
249	  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
250	\end{definition}
251	
252	\section{Immediate corollaries}
253	
254	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
255	  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
257	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles}
258	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
259	  $\bar k$. Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
260	  $0 \in \Div(C)$ is canonically the structure sheaf:
261	  \[
262	    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
263	  \]
264	  as sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$, equivalently as
265	  objects in $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
266	\end{lemma}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
270	  At $D = 0$ the coefficient $n_Q = 0$ at every prime divisor $Q$, so the
271	  section condition of \Cref{def:sheafOf} reduces, on every open
272	  $U \subseteq C$, to
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
275	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
276	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
277	  \]
278	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
279	  scheme $C$ (the regular functions on $U$ are exactly the rational
280	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
281	  $C$ landing in $U$; this is the standard ``non-negative order $=$
282	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
283	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
284	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$. The restriction maps on both sides are
285	  the identity on $K(C)$, so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
286	  equality of sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$.
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
290	\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}]
291	  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
292	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
293	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
294	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles}
295	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
296	  $\bar k$ and let $P \in C$ be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
297	  $[P] \in \Div(C)$ via \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. Then the
298	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$ of \Cref{def:sheafOf} agrees on
299	  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
300	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ of \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}:
301	  \[
302	    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
303	    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
304	  \]
305	  as sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$, equivalently as
306	  objects in $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
311	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point}
312	  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
313	  $[P]$ has coefficients $n_Q = 1$ if $Q = P$ and $n_Q = 0$ otherwise
314	  (where $P$ is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
315	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}). Thus
316	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ contains $0$ and those
317	  $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ satisfying $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$ for every prime
318	  divisor $Q \in U$ with $Q \neq P$, plus $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$ if
319	  $P \in U$.
320	
321	  On the \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} side, the same conditions
322	  are exactly the ones extracted by
323	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} of
324	  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open $U$ (not only at the global section
325	  $U = C$): a rational function $f \in K(C)$ is a section of
326	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ on $U$ iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
327	  $Q \in U \setminus \{P\}$ and has at most a simple pole at $P$ when
328	  $P \in U$.
329	
330	  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on $K(C)$. The two
331	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$ are therefore equal
332	  section-by-section as subsets of $K(C)$, and the equality propagates
333	  to an equality of sheaves of $\mathcal O_C$-modules. The
334	  $\bar k$-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
335	  $\bar k$-module structure on $K(C)$, so the identification is
336	  $\bar k$-linear on each section.
337	\end{proof}
338	
339	\begin{lemma}[Short exact sequence for $D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]$]
340	  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
342	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,
343	        def:divisor_closed_point}
344	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
345	  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any
346	  % point...''). Read from
347	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
348	  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
349	  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
350	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its
351	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$,
352	  % which we denote by $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$
353	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
354	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
355	  % Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
356	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$.
357	  % (Since $\mathscr L(D + P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
358	  % it does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$.)"
359	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
360	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
361	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
362	  $\bar k$, let $D \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor, and let $P \in C$ be
363	  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
364	  \Cref{def:prime_divisor} on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
365	  $\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ of
366	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$ fits into a short exact
367	  sequence
368	  \[
369	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
370	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
371	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
372	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
373	  \]
374	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ (equivalently, in
375	  $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$), where $k(P)$ denotes the
376	  skyscraper sheaf at $P$ with stalk $\bar k$ (the residue field of $P$
377	  is $\bar k$ since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $P$ is a
378	  $\bar k$-rational closed point).
379	\end{lemma}
380	
381	\begin{proof}
382	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
383	        lem:sheafOf_singlePoint, def:order_at_point}
384	  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
385	  proceeds in two beats:
386	
387	  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at $P$).} The closed-subscheme
388	  inclusion $P \hookrightarrow C$ has structure sheaf
389	  $\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)$ (a skyscraper at $P$ with stalk $\bar k$),
390	  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
391	  $\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])$ (the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of
392	  $\mathcal K_C$ generated by a local uniformiser $\pi_P$ at $P$ and by
393	  $1$ away from $P$, i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
394	  principal closed subscheme $P$). This produces the structural short
395	  exact sequence
396	  \[
397	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
398	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
399	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
400	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
401	  \]
402	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$.
403	
404	  \emph{Beat 2 (tensor with $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$).} The line bundle
405	  $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is locally free of rank~$1$
406	  (Hartshorne~II.6.13(a), \Cref{def:sheafOf}), so $- \otimes_{\mathcal O_C}
407	  \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is exact on $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ and preserves
408	  the short exact sequence of Beat~1:
409	  \[
410	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes
411	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
412	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
413	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
414	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
415	  \]
416	  Three identifications collapse this to the claimed sequence:
417	  \begin{itemize}
418	    \item $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
419	      \mathcal O_C(D)$ (tensor product of line bundles is the
420	      $\Div(C)$-additive operation $D' + D'' \mapsto \mathcal O_C(D' +
421	      D'')$ via Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(b), which is in turn the
422	      ring-theoretic identity
423	      $\pi_P^{-1} \cdot \pi_P^{1 + n_P^{D}} \cdot \prod_{Q \neq P}
424	      \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}} = \prod_Q \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}}$ on the generating
425	      rational functions, where $n_Q^{D}$ is the coefficient of $Q$
426	      in $D$);
427	    \item $\mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
428	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (the structure sheaf is the tensor unit);
429	    \item $k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong k(P)$ (tensoring a
430	      skyscraper at $P$ by a locally free rank-$1$ sheaf preserves the
431	      skyscraper; concretely, at the stalk over $P$ the line bundle
432	      $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])_P$ is a free $\mathcal O_{C,P}$-module of
433	      rank one, and $k(P) \otimes_{\mathcal O_{C,P}} \mathcal O_{C,P}
434	      \cong k(P)$ is just $k(P)$; away from $P$ the skyscraper is the
435	      zero sheaf, on which the tensor is the zero sheaf).
436	  \end{itemize}
437	  Substituting yields
438	  \[
439	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
440	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
441	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
442	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
443	  \]
444	  exact in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$, as required.
445	
446	  \emph{Compatibility with the structural inclusion.} The first map
447	  $\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is the
448	  inclusion of subsheaves of $\mathcal K_C$: at the level of sections,
449	  the constraint $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}$ of
450	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ implies the weaker constraint
451	  $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D} - 1$ of $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))$
452	  when $P \in U$ (constraints on $Q \neq P$ are unchanged), and the map
453	  is identity on $K(C)$ at the carrier level. The second map
454	  $\mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \twoheadrightarrow k(P)$ is the evaluation
455	  ``leading coefficient at the pole at $P$'' (i.e.\ the
456	  $\pi_P^{-(n_P^{D} + 1)}$-coefficient in the Laurent expansion of $f$
457	  at $P$), which is well-defined on $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))$
458	  modulo the subgroup of sections with $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}$ ---
459	  i.e.\ modulo $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$.
460	\end{proof}
461	
462	\section{Sheaf-property correctness}
463	
464	The construction of \Cref{def:sheafOf} is presheaf-by-hand: a
465	per-open $\bar k$-submodule of $K(C)$, with restriction along
466	$V \hookrightarrow U$ given by the identity on $K(C)$. This section
467	sketches the verifications that make the construction land in the
468	target category $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
469	
470	\paragraph{Functoriality of the carrier.} For $V \subseteq U$, the
471	constraint set defining $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ is a \emph{subset}
472	of the constraint set defining $\Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))$ (fewer
473	prime divisors to constrain at), so the identity on $K(C)$ restricts
474	to a well-defined inclusion
475	$\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))$.
476	Composition $W \subseteq V \subseteq U$ is again identity-on-$K(C)$, so
477	strict functoriality holds.
478	
479	\paragraph{$\bar k$-linearity.} A $\bar k$-scalar $\lambda \in \bar k$
480	acts on $K(C)$ by multiplication, and
481	$\ord_Q(\lambda \cdot f) = \ord_Q(f)$ for $\lambda \neq 0$
482	(units have order zero); for $\lambda = 0$, $\lambda \cdot f = 0$ is
483	included by convention. Thus the constraint set is a
484	$\bar k$-submodule and the restriction maps are $\bar k$-linear.
485	
486	\paragraph{Sheaf property.} The order condition
487	``$\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q$ for every prime divisor $Q \in U$'' is
488	\emph{stalk-local} at each $Q$: it depends only on the germ of $f$ at
489	the point of $C$ underlying $Q$, equivalently on the image of $f$ in
490	the DVR $\mathcal O_{C, Q}$. Consequently the sheaf property reduces
491	to two ingredients:
492	\begin{enumerate}
493	  \item \emph{The presheaf-of-sets underlying $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is a
494	    subpresheaf of the constant presheaf at $K(C)$.} The constant
495	    presheaf at $K(C)$ is a sheaf (every locally-constant section of a
496	    constant presheaf on a topological space is globally constant when
497	    the space is irreducible, which the topological space underlying an
498	    integral scheme is). A subpresheaf of a sheaf is itself a sheaf iff
499	    its membership condition is local; the order conditions are local
500	    at each prime divisor's underlying point, so the membership
501	    condition is local across an open cover and a section glued from
502	    locally-defined sections inherits the order condition at every
503	    prime divisor.
504	  \item \emph{The forget-functor bridge from
505	    $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$ to
506	    $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Set})$.} The categorical forget
507	    $\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k} \to \mathbf{Set}$ creates limits, hence
508	    reflects the sheaf property: a presheaf of $\bar k$-modules is a
509	    sheaf iff its underlying presheaf of sets is. Combined with~(i),
510	    this delivers the sheaf property for the
511	    $\bar k$-module-valued $\mathcal O_C(D)$.
512	\end{enumerate}
513	
514	\paragraph{Invertibility.} On any open $U \subseteq C$ disjoint from
515	the support of $D$, the constraint set is exactly the structure-sheaf
516	constraint $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$ for every prime divisor $Q \in U$; hence
517	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U = \mathcal O_C|_U$, and the trivialisation
518	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \cong \mathcal O_C|_U$ is the identity. On a small
519	open $U \subseteq C$ containing exactly one prime divisor $Q$ from the
520	support of $D$, the local generator $\pi_Q^{-n_Q} \in K(C)$ trivialises
521	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U$ as a free rank-$1$ $\mathcal O_C|_U$-module: a
522	section $f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ satisfies $\ord_Q(f) \geq
523	-n_Q$ iff $\pi_Q^{n_Q} \cdot f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$, and the
524	``multiplication by $\pi_Q^{n_Q}$'' map is an
525	$\mathcal O_C|_U$-linear isomorphism
526	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal O_C|_U$. The two types
527	of trivialising opens cover $C$ (any prime divisor lies in at most
528	finitely many supports across a covering, and the support of $D$ is
529	finite); patching the trivialisations on overlaps amounts to changing
530	uniformiser, which is unit-times the original (by the DVR property), so
531	the cocycle is in $\mathcal O_C^{\times}$ and the sheaf is invertible.
532	
533	The closure of (i)+(ii) in Lean follows the project's existing
534	\texttt{Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf} /
535	\texttt{toModuleKPresheaf\_isSheaf} template in
536	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/}, which
537	landed the analogous bottom-up
538	$\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$-valued construction for the
539	structure sheaf. The order-condition locality is closed via the project's
540	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} stalk bridge of
541	\texttt{RR.1} (\Cref{def:order_at_point} and surrounding lemmas).
542

582	\section{Out of scope}
583	
584	\begin{itemize}
585	  \item \textbf{Linear-equivalence isomorphism.} The natural
586	    isomorphism $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when
587	    $D \sim D'$ (i.e.\ $D - D' = \div(f)$ for some
588	    $f \in K(C)^{\times}$) is \emph{not} formalised in this chapter.
589	    It is not needed for the genus-$0$ critical path: \texttt{RR.2}
590	    consumes only $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D))$ and the SES additivity at
591	    closed points; \texttt{RR.3} consumes only the closed-point
592	    specialisation; \texttt{RR.4} consumes the function-field /
593	    degree bridge, which is divisorial in $\Div(C)$ rather than
594	    in $\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \Div(C) / \sim$. The
595	    Cartier-divisor $\leftrightarrow$ \texttt{Pic} isomorphism is
596	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter.
597	  \item \textbf{Sheaf-multiplication
598	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D') \cong \mathcal O_C(D +
599	    D')$.} The tensor-product identity of Hartshorne's
600	    Proposition~6.13(b) is invoked inside the proof of
601	    \Cref{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add} at the single instance
602	    $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
603	    \mathcal O_C(D)$, but the \emph{statement} of
604	    Proposition~6.13(b) as a separate lemma is not formalised in this
605	    chapter. The instance used inside the SES proof is local to that
606	    proof; a generalised tensor-additivity lemma is queued for the
607	    post-RR-bridge follow-up.
608	  \item \textbf{Pullback of $\mathcal O_C(D)$ along a morphism.} The
609	    pullback $f^* \mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_{C'}(f^* D)$ for
610	    a morphism of curves $f : C' \to C$, used in degree-computation
611	    arguments on a base change, is \emph{not} formalised in this
612	    chapter and is not needed for the genus-$0$ critical path.
613	  \item \textbf{Global-sections identification as the Riemann--Roch
614	    space.} The identification
615	    $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \cong L(D)$ for a general Weil divisor
616	    $D$ (i.e.\ the analogue of
617	    \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} for general
618	    $D$) is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up. The closed-point
619	    case $D = [P]$ is the only specialisation consumed by the
620	    genus-$0$ critical path, and it is handled in
621	    \texttt{RR.3} via \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}.
622	  \item \textbf{Serre duality and the canonical sheaf.} As recorded in
623	    the \texttt{RR.2} and \texttt{RR.3} chapters, Mathlib (snapshot
624	    \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme
625	    and no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ critical path
626	    does \emph{not} require either; the present chapter accordingly
627	    does not engage them.
628	\end{itemize}

11	# The Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.2** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
14	headline `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically
15	irreducible genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` / `WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil
22	  divisor group `Div(C)` and the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
23	- **`RR.2` (this file, `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`)**: the Riemann–Roch
24	  dimension formula `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0` (with `deg D ≥ 0`),
25	  via the auxiliary Euler-characteristic identity
26	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`.
27	- `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, future): the invertible sheaf
28	  `𝒪_C(D)`, the linear-equivalence isomorphism `𝒪_C(D) ≅ 𝒪_C(D')` for
29	  `D ∼ D'`, and the `H¹`-vanishing input
30	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0` for `deg D ≥ 0` on a genus-`0` curve.
31	- `RR.4` (`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`, future): the "two-section
32	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
33

35	
36	This file is the **iter-174 Lane F** file-skeleton: each of the four pinned

100	/-! ## §1. The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve -/
101	
102	/-- **Euler characteristic of a `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf on a smooth proper
103	curve `C / k̄`.**
104	
105	On a curve (one-dimensional scheme), Grothendieck vanishing
106	(Hartshorne III.2.7) gives `H^i(C, 𝓕) = 0` for `i ≥ 2`, so the classical
107	alternating sum
108	`χ(𝓕) = Σ_{i ≥ 0} (-1)^i dim_{k̄} H^i(C, 𝓕)` collapses to the two-term
109	expression
110	`χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)`.
111	
112	This is the definition we ship. Coherence of `𝓕` on the proper `k̄`-scheme
113	`C` guarantees that both `H⁰` and `H¹` are finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector
114	spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem, the same
115	finiteness backing `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`), so the two `Module.finrank`s
116	are honest natural numbers and the difference is a well-defined integer.
117	
118	The `Module k̄`-valued cohomology pipeline is the project's
119	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009), the same wrapper used by
120	`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`.
121	
122	Blueprint reference: `def:eulerChar_curve`
123	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295, displayed inside the proof of Theorem 1.3). -/
124	noncomputable def Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
125	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
126	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
127	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
128	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
129	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
130	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : ℤ :=
131	  (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 0) : ℤ)
132	    - (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 1) : ℤ)
133	
134	/-! ## §2. The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor (placeholder)
135	
136	The honest construction of `𝒪_C(D)` lives in the sibling chapter `RR.3`
137	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`), where the locally-principal ideal sheaves of
138	closed points are glued into an invertible `𝒪_C`-module. To keep the
139	iter-174 skeleton's pin signatures substantive, we expose `𝒪_C(D)` as a
140	typed-`sorry` `ModuleCat k̄`-valued sheaf-on-`C`. The iter-175+ closure of
141	`RR.3` will replace the body with the construction; downstream pins (the
142	`ℓ`-invariant, the χ-identity, the genus-`0` Riemann–Roch formula) consume
143	this placeholder only through its `H⁰` and `H¹`, so their signatures
144	encode arithmetic identities on finiteness-of-cohomology outputs and are
145	not vacuous. -/
146	
147	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
148	
149	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
150	  {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
151	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
152	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
153	
154	/-- **The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper
155	curve `C / k̄`** (placeholder for `RR.3`).
156	
157	For a Weil divisor `D = Σᵢ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` on a smooth proper geometrically
158	irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the associated invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` is the
159	locally-free `𝒪_C`-module of rank one cut out (locally near each `Pᵢ`) by
160	the `(−nᵢ)`-th power of the local uniformiser at `Pᵢ`. Hartshorne II §6
161	Proposition 6.13 packages this construction; the project-side `RR.3`
162	chapter (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, to be added) will produce the carrier as
163	a `Sheaf` of `ModuleCat k̄`-modules on `C.left`.
164	
165	In the iter-174 skeleton this is a typed-`sorry`. The body in `RR.3` will
166	be the invertible-sheaf glue of the locally principal ideals of the
167	support of `D`. -/
168	noncomputable def sheafOf (_D : C.left.WeilDivisor) :
169	    Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
170	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :=
171	  sorry
172	
173	/-! ## §3. The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor -/
174	
175	/-- **The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve
176	`C / k̄`.**
177	
178	By definition,
179	`ℓ(D) := dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D)) ∈ ℕ`,
180	the `k̄`-dimension of the space of global sections of the invertible sheaf
181	`𝒪_C(D)` associated to `D`. Finiteness of `ℓ(D)` is a consequence of the
182	coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem on a proper `k̄`-scheme
183	(Hartshorne II.5.19 / III.5.2), the same input that backs the finiteness
184	of `genus C`.
185	
186	The classical *complete linear system* `|D|` has `k̄`-projective dimension
187	`ℓ(D) − 1`; the chapter never uses this projective interpretation —
188	`ℓ(D)` is the only quantity consumed by the Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain.
189	
190	Blueprint reference: `def:l_invariant` (Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295,
191	"We denote `dim_k H⁰(X, 𝒪(D))` by `l(D)`"). -/
192	noncomputable def l (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) : ℕ :=
193	  Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf (C := C) D) 0)
194	
195	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
196	
197	/-! ## §4. The χ-identity: `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`
198	
199	The bridge from the structural Euler characteristic to the arithmetic
200	degree of a divisor. Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3 reduces Riemann–Roch to
201	this identity; the proof is the inductive `D ↔ D + [P]` step (additivity
202	of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence, with base case `D = 0`
203	giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` from `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` and the definition of
204	the genus). The closure of the body is iter-175+ work after the
205	`Euler-characteristic additivity on a short exact sequence`-style
206	project-side helper is supplied and the `RR.3` sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` has a
207	body.
208	
209	**Iter-181 Lane H factoring.** Both Hartshorne IV.1.3 inputs (the base
210	case `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` and the inductive step `χ(𝒪_C(D + Y)) = χ(𝒪_C(D))
211	+ n`) intrinsically reference the body of `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf`,
212	which is still a typed-`sorry` placeholder waiting on the sibling
213	chapter `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`). We therefore factor the proof
214	into two named substantive helper lemmas (3-tier disclosure: **honest
215	named-sorry helpers** — each helper has a substantive type encoding a
216	nontrivial mathematical claim about the `sheafOf` line bundle whose
217	closure is downstream of the `RR.3` body):
218	
219	1. `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero` — base case `χ(sheafOf 0) = 1 − g`,
220	   which on closure of `RR.3` reduces to `sheafOf 0 = toModuleKSheaf C`
221	   plus the Hartshorne I.3.4 input `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` plus the
222	   definition of the genus.
223	2. `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add` — inductive step
224	   `χ(sheafOf (Finsupp.single Y n + D)) = χ(sheafOf D) + n` for any
225	   `Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor` and `n : ℤ`. On closure of `RR.3` this
226	   reduces to the Hartshorne IV.1.3 SES additivity argument iterated
227	   `|n|` times (with sign for `n < 0`).
228	
229	The main theorem then closes by induction on the `Finsupp`-structure of
230	`D : C.left.WeilDivisor` (via `Finsupp.induction`), using the two
231	helpers and the additivity of `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree`. -/
232	
233	/-- **Base case of the χ-identity** (Lane H helper 1/2): on a smooth
234	proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the Euler characteristic
235	of the structure-sheaf-side line bundle `sheafOf 0` is `1 − g(C)`. -/
236	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero
237	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
238	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
239	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
240	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left] :
241	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
242	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) (0 : C.left.WeilDivisor))
243	      = 1 - (AlgebraicGeometry.genus C : ℤ) := by
244	  -- On closure of `RR.3`, `sheafOf 0 ≅ toModuleKSheaf C` (the structure
245	  -- sheaf), so `χ(sheafOf 0) = dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) - dim H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`.
246	  -- Hartshorne I.3.4 gives `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` (a proper geometrically
247	  -- irreducible variety over an algebraically closed field has
248	  -- `H⁰(𝒪) = k`); the definition of `genus` gives
249	  -- `dim H¹(C, 𝒪_C) = g(C)`. Substituting yields `1 - g`. The full
250	  -- closure waits on `RR.3` supplying a body for `sheafOf`.
251	  sorry
252	
253	/-- **Inductive step of the χ-identity** (Lane H helper 2/2): on a smooth
254	proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the Euler characteristic
255	of `sheafOf` transports across the elementary modification
256	`D ↦ Finsupp.single Y n + D` with arithmetic increment `n`. The argument
257	`D` carries the underlying `Finsupp` type rather than `Scheme.WeilDivisor`
258	so that the `Finsupp.single Y n + D` term elaborates cleanly; the result
259	is consumed via the definitional equality `WeilDivisor = (PrimeDivisor →₀
260	ℤ)`. -/
261	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add
262	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
263	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
264	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
265	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
266	    (D : C.left.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) (Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ) :
267	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
268	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) (Finsupp.single Y n + D))
269	      = Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
270	          (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D) + n := by
271	  -- For `n ≥ 0`, iterate the Hartshorne IV.1.3 closed-point SES
272	  --   `0 → 𝒪_C(D + k·Y) → 𝒪_C(D + (k+1)·Y) → k(Y) → 0`
273	  -- `n` times, using additivity of χ on SES and `χ(k(Y)) = 1`.
274	  -- For `n < 0` apply the same argument to `-n` and use additivity
275	  -- of `sheafOf` under the inverse line bundle. The full closure waits
276	  -- on `RR.3` supplying a body for `sheafOf` and the project-side
277	  -- helper for SES additivity of χ (Mathlib gap: no
278	  -- `CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar_additive` under that name —
279	  -- see blueprint chapter §4 Lean reference note).
280	  sorry
281	
282	/-- **Euler-characteristic identity for `𝒪_C(D)` on a smooth proper curve
283	of genus `g`.**
284	
285	For every Weil divisor `D ∈ Div(C)`,
286	`χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g(C)`.
287	
288	The proof is Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3's reduction: induction on the
289	free-abelian-group structure of `Div(C)` on closed points, base case `D =
290	0` giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` (the `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` is the Hartshorne
291	I.3.4 input via the project's `H⁰`-bridge), and inductive step via
292	additivity of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence
293	`0 → 𝒪_C(D) → 𝒪_C(D + [P]) → k(P) → 0`.
294	
295	Blueprint reference: `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
296	(Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3, p. 295).
297	
298	**Iter-181 Lane H status** — the main theorem is closed by induction on
299	the `Finsupp` structure of `D`, consuming the two `sheafOf`-side helper
300	sorries `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero` (base case) and

15

20	Hartshorne's condition `(*)`, the principal-divisor homomorphism

22	proper curve over an algebraically closed field, the degree-zero of principal divisors
23	on a complete nonsingular curve (Hartshorne Cor. II.6.10), and the linear-equivalence
